revision.text:
[[ca:Clan Abe]][[de:Abe (Klan)]][[en:Abe clan]][[es:Clan Abe]][[eu:Abe klana]][[fa:خاندان آبه]][[fr:Clan Abe]][[it:Clan Abe]][[ja:安倍氏 (奥州)]][[pt:Clã Abe]][[ru:Абэ (род)]][[uk:Рід Абе]][[zh:安倍氏 (奥州)]] {{Short description|Major Japanese clan|qq=}} {{About|the historical Japanese clan|contemporary Japanese political family|Satō–Kishi–Abe family|other uses|Abe clan (disambiguation)|qq=}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Abe |surname nihongo = 安倍 |image = Japanese crest Abe Takanoha.svg |caption = |home province = {{ill|Iga Province|en|Iga Province|qid=Q1047128|lt=Iga|1=Iga Province}}
{{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|qid=Q907389|lt=Mutsu|1=Mutsu Province}}
{{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa|1=Dewa Province}} |titles = Various |founder={{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|lt=Prince Ōhiko|ja|大彦命|12=simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōhiko|13=User:Immanuelle/simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōhiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōhiko|qq=}}|cadet branches={{ill|Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado clan|lt=Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家|12=simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|13=User:Immanuelle/simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuchimikado family|qq=}}|qq=}} The {{nihongo|'''Abe clan'''|安倍氏|Abe-shi|qq=}} was one of the oldest of the major [[Japanese clans]] (''uji''); and the clan retained its prominence during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|qid=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} and the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}.Meyer, Eva-Maria. [https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/manabu/shoshidai.htm "Gouverneure von Kyôto in der Edo-Zeit."] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080411113817/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/manabu/shoshidai.htm |date=2008-04-11 |qq=}} Universität Tübingen (in German). The clan's origin is said to be one of the original clans of the {{ill|Yamato people|en|Yamato people|qid=Q640605|lt=Yamato people|1=Yamato people}}; they truly gained prominence during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} (794-1185), and experienced a resurgence in the 18th century. Although {{ill|Abe (surname)|en|Abe (surname)|qid=Q11160829|lt=Abe|1=Abe (surname)}} is also a very common Japanese surname in modern times, not everyone with this name is descended from this clan. ==Origins and history== According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', the Abe were descended from {{ill|Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|lt=Ōhiko-no-Mikoto|lt=Prince Ōhiko|ja|大彦命|12=simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōhiko|13=User:Immanuelle/simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōhiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Ōhiko|qq=}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|qid=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}}.{{ill|Kan'ichi Asakawa|en|Kan'ichi Asakawa|qid=Q3138143|lt=Asakawa, Kan'ichi|1=Kan'ichi Asakawa}}. (1903). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan,'' p.140. They originated in {{ill|Iga Province|en|Iga Province|qid=Q1047128|lt=Iga Province|1=Iga Province}} (today [[:en:Mie prefecture|Mie prefecture]]); Though the clan name was originally written as 阿倍, it changed to 安倍 around the 8th century. Though this origin is not positive, it is likely. The northern region which would come to be known as the provinces of {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|qid=Q907389|lt=Mutsu|1=Mutsu Province}} and {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa|1=Dewa Province}}, was conquered by the Japanese sometime in the 9th century, and the native {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|qid=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} people there subjugated or displaced. While many provinces at this time were overseen primarily by a governor, Mutsu saw to the rise of independent families called [[gōzoku]] which administered local affairs. The Abe were appointed as "Superintendent of the Aborigines" ostensibly to control the local people who by now were a mix of Japanese immigrants and former Emishi tribesmen on behalf of the central government, but in reality the government in Kyōto simply did not have control over the region, and was recognizing this fact by appointing the Abe. The Abe for their part used their position to take control over the so-called six districts ''roku-oku-gun'' located in what is now central Iwate prefecture surrounding the Kitakami river. In time, they began to have disputes with the governor of Mutsu, an office held by a branch of the Fujiwara family, which erupted into violence in 1051. The main reason given for the attack on the Abe was that they stopped paying taxes to Kyoto, and stopped contributing to the local government. The governors of Mutsu and the commander of Dewa fort combined their forces to attack the Abe, but were defeated. Desperate to quell this affront to their authority Kyōto appointed Minamoto Yoriyoshi as ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. The position known as ''Chinjufu-shōgun'', or "Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North", was traditionally given by the court as a temporary appointment to a courtier (typically of high Rank) who was appointed as a national general to quell uprisings among the ''Emishi'' or ''Ebisu'' barbarians of northern Honshū. Increasingly, as military power became privatized, this position was rotated among a few clans. In what has come to be termed the "Earlier Nine Years' War" (前九年合戦, ''[[:en:Zenkunen War|Zenkunen kassen]]''), Abe Yoritoki was killed, and his son {{ill|Abe no Sadato|en|Abe no Sadato|qid=Q3616500|lt=Abe no Sadato|1=Abe no Sadato}} defeated, by Minamoto no Yoriyoshi and ''his'' son, {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|qid=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}}. This war broke the power of the Abe family, but in the prolonged fighting that took place the Minamoto would not have prevailed had it not been for the aid of another powerful family, the Kiyowara. The [[:en:Kiyowara clan|Kiyowara clan]] of nearby {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|qid=Q876032|lt=Dewa Province|1=Dewa Province}}, aided the Minamoto in defeating the Abe.{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |isbn=9781590207307 |pages=95–109|qq=}}{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=249–251|qq=}} ===Other Abe families=== Though many other major figures throughout history have been called Abe, it is difficult to know which were related to the Abe clan of Iga and Mutsu. {{ill|Abe no Nakamaro|en|Abe no Nakamaro|qid=Q479788|lt=Abe no Nakamaro|1=Abe no Nakamaro}}, a major court noble of the 8th century, for example, was from the town of Abe, near {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, and derived his family name thus. A family by the name of Abe also proved significant during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, serving successively in the post of ''[[Rōjū]]'', or Elders, who advised the [[:en:Tokugawa shōgun|Tokugawa shōgun]]. Again, it is difficult to determine whether or not this line was directly related to the much earlier Abe clan, but it is of an importance itself nevertheless. {{ill|Abe Tadaaki|en|Abe Tadaaki|qid=Q4666449|lt=Abe Tadaaki|1=Abe Tadaaki}} was the first to serve as ''Rōjū'', holding the post from 1633-71. He was very likely a son or other direct relation to {{ill|Abe Masatsugu|en|Abe Masatsugu|qid=Q4666427|lt=Abe Masatsugu|1=Abe Masatsugu}} (1569–1647) who served {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and fought under him at the decisive {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}. Other members of the Abe family would succeed Tadaaki to the post for much of the Edo period (1603–1867), ending with {{ill|Abe Masahiro|en|Abe Masahiro|qid=Q1070088|lt=Abe Masahiro|1=Abe Masahiro}}, who was chief of the Council of ''Rōjū'' at the time of the arrival of [[Matthew Perry (naval officer)|Commodore Perry]]. ==Clan members of note== *{{ill|Abe no Hirafu|en|Abe no Hirafu|qid=Q479775|lt=Abe no Hirafu|1=Abe no Hirafu}} (c. 575-664), also known as Abe no Ōmi, one of the leading generals in the subjugation of the Ainu *{{ill|Abe no Yoritoki|en|Abe no Yoritoki|qid=Q479780|lt=Abe no Yoritoki|1=Abe no Yoritoki}} (died 1057) - ''[[:en:Chinjufu-shōgun|Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' during the [[:en:Zenkunen War|Zenkunen War]] *{{ill|Abe no Sadato|en|Abe no Sadato|qid=Q3616500|lt=Abe no Sadato|1=Abe no Sadato}} (1019–62) *{{ill|Abe Masatsugu|en|Abe Masatsugu|qid=Q4666427|lt=Abe Masatsugu|1=Abe Masatsugu}} (1569–1647) - fought at Sekigahara, became a ''[[:en:Fudai|Fudai]]'' daimyō under the Tokugawa *{{ill|Abe Tadaaki|en|Abe Tadaaki|qid=Q4666449|lt=Abe Tadaaki|1=Abe Tadaaki}} - first Abe clan member of the ''Rōjū'' *{{ill|Abe Masahiro|en|Abe Masahiro|qid=Q1070088|lt=Abe Masahiro|1=Abe Masahiro}} - among the last of the ''Rōjū'', signed [[:en:Treaty of Kanagawa|Treaty of Kanagawa]] *[[Abe no Seimei]] - famed practitioner of ''[[onmyōdō]]'' ==Genealogy== {{Generations of Jimmu|qq=}} ==Notes== {{notelist|qq=}} ==References== ===Citations=== {{reflist|qq=}} ===Books=== * Appert, Georges and H. Kinoshita. (1888). [https://books.google.com/books?id=HYc_AAAAMAAJ&q=ancien+japon ''Ancien Japon.''] Tokyo: Imprimerie Kokubunsha. * {{ill|Kan'ichi Asakawa|en|Kan'ichi Asakawa|qid=Q3138143|lt=Asakawa, Kan'ichi|1=Kan'ichi Asakawa}}. (1903). [https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_K1MuAAAAYAAJ ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: {{ill|Shueisha|en|Shueisha|qid=Q844822|lt=Shueisha|1=Shueisha}}. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org] * {{ill|Louis Frédéric|en|Louis Frédéric|qid=Q726040|lt=Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric|1=Louis Frédéric}}. (2002). [https://books.google.com/books?id=y0zGAAAACAAJ&q=Japan+Encyclopedia ''Japan Encyclopedia.''] Cambridge, Massachusetts: {{ill|Harvard University Press|en|Harvard University Press|qid=Q1587900|lt=Harvard University Press|1=Harvard University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-674-00770-3|qq=}} * [[:en:Edmund Papinot|Papinot, Edmund]]. (1906) ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du japon.'' Tokyo: Librarie Sansaisha.[http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf ..Click link for digitized 1906 ''Nobiliaire du japon'' (2003)] * [[:en:George Bailey Sansom|Sansom, George Bailey]]. (1958). A History of Japan to 1334. Stanford: {{ill|Stanford University Press|en|Stanford University Press|qid=Q1479937|lt=Stanford University Press|1=Stanford University Press}}. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0523-3|qq=}} * ____________. (1961). ''A History of Japan: 1334-1615.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0525-7|qq=}} * ____________. (1963). ''A History of Japan: 1615-1867.'' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-8047-0527-1|qq=}} * {{ill|Stephen Turnbull (historian)|en|Stephen Turnbull (historian)|qid=Q3176384|lt=Turnbull, Stephen R.|1=Stephen Turnbull (historian)}} (1998). The Samurai Sourcebook. London: Arms & Armour Press. {{ISBN|1-85409-371-1|qq=}}; reprinted by Cassell & Co., London, 2000. {{ISBN|1-85409-523-4|qq=}} [[en:Abe clan]] [[ca:Clan Abe]] [[de:Abe (Klan)]] [[es:Clan Abe]] [[eu:Abe klana]] [[fa:خاندان آبه]] [[fr:Clan Abe]] [[it:Clan Abe]] [[ja:安倍氏 (奥州)]] [[pt:Clã Abe]] [[ru:Абэ (род)]] [[uk:Рід Абе]] {{wikidata link|Q2975203}} [[Category:奥六郡]] [[Category:奥州安倍氏]] [[Category:奥州市の歴史]] [[Category:History of Iwate Prefecture]] [[Category:Heian period]] [[Category:Clans of Tōhoku Region]] [[Category:Uji]]
